sources.notes: Used to read the interactive Manhattan-distance reconstruction statement.
Used to confirm the corner-finding and two-anchor coordinate reconstruction.

reference_candidates.notes: 洛谷题解候选来源；只有实际参考后才移动到 sources。

annotation.summary: Reconstruct an n by n grid placement up to symmetry using Manhattan-distance queries between labeled penguins.
annotation.constraints: n <= 100, total n <= 500, and the query budget is 3n^2 + 150, so the method can query all labels from a constant number of anchors.
annotation.core_idea: A cell farthest from an arbitrary anchor is a corner. Distances from that corner identify the non-opposite-corner candidates at distance n-1; querying among those candidates finds a second non-opposite corner. Distances to two non-opposite corners uniquely determine each coordinate by solving two Manhattan-distance equations, and any reflected/rotated reconstruction is accepted.
tricks: find a corner as a farthest point
use two non-opposite corner distance signatures
solve coordinates from Manhattan distance equations

solution_variants.name: main
solution_variants.summary: Find two adjacent corners using farthest-distance queries, then locate every label from its two distances to those corners.

tags.evidence: The algorithm is constrained by an interactive query budget and reconstructs the hidden grid through distance queries.
The main reconstruction uses Manhattan geometry and distances from grid corners to derive coordinates.
Each penguin is identified by the pair of distances to two selected non-opposite corners.
tags.description: Recover or distinguish objects by a vector of distances to selected anchors.
tags.created_reason: This pattern recurs in reconstruction and interactive geometry tasks where selected anchors encode positions.

tags.evidence: Any grid consistent with all pairwise distances is valid, so the solution constructs one canonical orientation.
The editorial notes small constant query optimizations, but they are not the conceptual core.
